concepts.title: Methods implemented to solve Trigonometric limits when the independent variable tends to a finite real number
concepts.conceptBody:
Problems involving trigonometrical expressions

Working Rule: 

  1. First of all see whether independent variable tend to zero or not. If the independent variable $\text{x}\to \text{a}$, where $\text{a}\ne 0$, then put $\text{x}=\text{a}+\text{h}$. Then go on simplifying only those factors of the numerator and denominator which tend to zero till $\text{sin}\theta $ or $\text{tan}\theta $ occurs as a factor where $\theta \to 0$.
  2. Then write $\text{sin}\theta =\frac{\text{sin}\theta }{\theta }\cdot \theta $ and $\text{tan}\theta =\frac{\text{sin}\theta }{\theta }\cdot \theta $ and use the formula

$\underset{\theta \to 0}{\mathop{Lt}}\,\frac{sin\theta }{\theta }=1,\underset{\theta \to 0}{\mathop{Lt}}\,\frac{tan\theta }{\theta }=1$ whichever is required.

concepts.title: Solved problems on Trigonometric Limits when the independent variable tends to a finite real number
concepts.conceptBody:
Solved problems

(a) Find $\underset{\theta \to 0}{\mathop{Lt}}\,\frac{sina\theta }{tanb\theta }$ 
(b) $L{{t}_{x\to 0}}\frac{tan{{x}^{o}}}{tanx}$ 

Solution: 

(a) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\theta \to 0}}\frac{\text{sin}a\theta }{\text{tan}b\theta }=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\theta \to 0}}\frac{\left( \frac{\text{sin}a\theta }{a\theta } \right)a\theta }{\left( \frac{\text{tan}b\theta }{b\theta } \right)b\theta }$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\theta \to 0}}\frac{\left( \frac{\text{sin}a\theta }{a\theta } \right)a}{\left( \frac{\text{tan}b\theta }{b\theta } \right)b}=\frac{1\cdot a}{1.b}=\frac{a}{b}$

(b) ${{x}^{\circ }}=\frac{\pi }{180}\text{x}$ radian

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Now }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\text{tan}{{\text{x}}^{\circ }}}{\text{x}}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{x}\to 0}}\frac{\text{tan}\frac{\pi \text{x}}{180}}{\text{x}}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{x}\to 0}}\frac{\frac{\text{tan}\frac{\pi \text{x}}{180}}{\frac{\pi \text{x}}{180}}\cdot \frac{\pi \text{x}}{180}}{\text{x}}=\frac{\pi }{180}$

 

(a) Find $L{{t}_{x\to 0}}\frac{x\left( cosx+cos2x \right)}{sinx}$ 
(b) $L{{t}_{x\to 0}}\frac{cosx-cos3x}{x\left( sin3x-sinx \right)}$ 
(c) $\underset{x\to 0}{\mathop{Lt}}\,\frac{tanx-sinx}{1-cosx}$ 
(d) $li{{m}_{x\to \frac{\pi }{2}}}\frac{sin\left( cosx \right)cosx}{sinx-cosecx}$ 

Solution: 

(a) =$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{x\left( \text{cos}x+\text{cos}2x \right)}{\text{sin}x}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{x\left( \text{cos}x+\text{cos}2x \right)}{\frac{\text{sin}x}{x}\cdot x}$

[Here factor $\left( \text{cos}x+\text{cos}2x \right)$, does not tend to zero, hence it is not necessary to simplify it]

=$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\text{cos}x+\text{cos}2x}{\frac{\text{sin}x}{x}}=\frac{1+1}{1}=2$

(b) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\text{cos}x-\text{cos}3x}{x\left( \text{sin}3x-\text{sin}x \right)}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{2\text{sin}\frac{x+3x}{2}\text{sin}\frac{3x-x}{2}}{x\cdot 2\text{cos}\frac{x+3x}{2}\text{sin}\frac{3x-x}{2}}$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{2\text{sin}2x\text{sin}x}{2x\text{cos}2x\text{sin}x}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\text{sin}2x}{x\text{cos}2x}$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\frac{\text{sin}2x}{2x}\cdot 2x}{x\text{cos}2x}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\frac{\text{sin}2x}{2x}\cdot 2}{\text{cos}2x}=\frac{2}{1}=2$

(c) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\text{tan}x-\text{sin}x}{1-\text{cos}x}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\frac{\text{sin}x}{\text{cos}x}-\text{sin}x}{1-\text{cos}x}$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\text{sin}x-\text{sin}x\text{cos}x}{\text{cos}x\left( 1-\text{cos}x \right)}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\text{sin}x\left( 1-\text{cos}x \right)}{\text{cos}x\left( 1-\text{cos}x \right)}$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\text{tan}x=0$ [By the definition of limit because the form is not indeterminate]

(d) $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to \frac{\pi }{2}}}\frac{\text{sin}\left( \text{cos}x \right)\text{cos}x}{\text{sin}x-\text{cosec}x}$

$=\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to \frac{\pi }{2}}}\frac{\text{sin}\left( \text{cos}x \right)\text{cos}x\text{sin}x}{\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}x-1}$

=$\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to \frac{\pi }{2}}}-\frac{\left[ \text{sin}\left( \text{cos}x \right)\text{sin}x \right]}{\text{cos}x}=-\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to \frac{\pi }{2}}}\frac{\text{sin}\left( \text{cos}x \right)\text{sin}x}{\text{cos}x}$

as $x\to \frac{\pi }{2}\text{cos}x\to 0$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ we can use $\frac{\text{sinx}}{\text{x}}$ rule

$=\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to \frac{\pi }{2}}}\text{sinx}=-1$

 

$\left( {} \right.$ Using $\left. li{{m}_{x\to 0}}\frac{sinx}{x}=1 \right)$ Evaluate the following limits : 
(a) $li{{m}_{x\to \frac{\pi }{3}}}\frac{tanx-\sqrt{3}}{9{{x}^{2}}-{{\pi }^{2}}}$ 
(b) $\underset{x\to 0}{\mathop{Lt}}\,\frac{tanx-sinx}{{{x}^{3}}}$ 
(c) $\underset{x\to y}{\mathop{Lt}}\,\frac{tanx-tany}{x-y}$ 
(d) $li{{m}_{x\to a}}\frac{asinx-xsina}{a{{x}^{2}}-{{a}^{2}}x}$ 

Solution: 

(a) $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{\text{x}\to \frac{\pi }{3}}}\frac{\text{tanx}-\sqrt{3}}{9{{\text{x}}^{2}}-{{\pi }^{2}}}=\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{\text{x}\to \frac{\pi }{3}}}\frac{\text{tanx}-\text{tan}\frac{\pi }{3}}{9{{\text{x}}^{2}}-{{\pi }^{2}}}$

Using tan $A-\text{tan}B=\frac{\text{sin}\left( A-B \right)}{\text{cos}A\text{cos}B}$ we get,

$~\underset{x\to \frac{\pi }{3}}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{\text{sin}\left( x-\frac{\pi }{3} \right)}{\text{cos}x\text{cos}\frac{\pi }{3}\left( 3x-\pi \right)\left( 3x+\pi \right)}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {} \\ {} & ~=\frac{1}{3}\frac{1}{\text{cos}\frac{\pi }{3}\text{cos}\frac{\pi }{3}\left( \pi +\pi \right)}\left( \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ using }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\underset{\theta \to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{\text{sin}\theta }{\theta }=1 \right)=\frac{2}{3\pi } \\ \end{array}$

(b) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{x}\to 0}}\frac{\text{tanx}-\text{sinx}}{{{\text{x}}^{3}}}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{x}\to 0}}\frac{\frac{\text{sinx}}{\text{cosx}}-\text{sinx}}{{{\text{x}}^{3}}}$

$~=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\text{sin}x\left( 1-\text{cos}x \right)}{{{x}^{3}}\text{cos}x}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\text{sin}x\cdot 2\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\frac{x}{2}}{{{x}^{3}}\text{cos}x}$

$~=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{2\left( \frac{\text{sin}x}{x} \right)x\cdot {{\left( \frac{\text{sin}\frac{x}{2}}{\frac{x}{2}} \right)}^{2}}{{\left( \frac{x}{2} \right)}^{2}}}{{{x}^{3}}\text{cos}x}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{2\left( \frac{\text{sin}x}{x} \right)x\cdot {{\left( \frac{\text{sin}\frac{x}{2}}{\frac{x}{2}} \right)}^{2}}\cdot \frac{1}{4}}{\text{cos}x}=\frac{2.1\cdot {{1}^{2}}\frac{1}{4}}{1}=\frac{1}{2}$

(c) [Here independent variable $\text{x}$ is not tending to zero rather $\text{x}$ is tending to $\text{y}$, hence put $\text{x}=\text{y}+\text{h}]$

Let $\text{x}=\text{y}+\text{h}$, then as $\text{x}\to \text{y},\text{h}\to 0$

Now $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{x}\to \text{y}}}\frac{\text{tanx}-\text{tany}}{\text{x}-\text{y}}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{h}\to 0}}\frac{\text{tan}\left( \text{y}+\text{h} \right)-\text{tany}}{\text{y}+\text{h}-\text{y}}$

=$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{h}\to 0}}\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}}\left[ \frac{\text{sin}\left( \text{y}+\text{h} \right)}{\text{cos}\left( \text{y}+\text{h} \right)}-\frac{\text{siny}}{\text{cosy}} \right]$

=$\underset{\text{h}\to \text{0}}{\mathop{\text{Lt}}}\,\frac{\text{sin}\left( \text{y}+\text{h} \right)\text{cosy}-\text{cos}\left( \text{y}+\text{h} \right)\text{siny}}{\text{hcos}\left( \text{y}+\text{h} \right)\text{cosy}}$

=$\underset{\text{h}\to 0}{\mathop{\text{Lt}}}\,\frac{\text{sin}\left( \text{y}+\text{h}-\text{y} \right)}{\text{hcos}\left( \text{y}+\text{h} \right)\text{cosy}}$

=$\underset{h\to 0}{\mathop{\text{Lt}}}\,\frac{\text{sinh}}{\text{h}}\cdot \frac{1}{\text{cos}\left( \text{y}+\text{h} \right)\text{cosy}}=1\frac{1}{\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\text{y}}=\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}\text{y}$

(d) $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to a}}\frac{a\text{sin}x-x\text{sin}a}{a{{x}^{2}}-{{a}^{2}}x}$

$~=\underset{x\to a}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{a\text{sin}x-x\text{sin}x+x\text{sin}x-x\text{sin}a}{ax\left( x-a \right)}$

$~=\underset{x\to a}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{\left( a-x \right)\text{sin}x+x\left( \text{sin}x-\text{sin}a \right)}{ax\left( x-a \right)}$

$~=\underset{x\to a}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{\left( a-x \right)\text{sin}x}{ax\left( x-a \right)}+\underset{x\to a}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{\text{sin}x-\text{sin}a}{a\left( x-a \right)}$

$~=-\frac{\text{sin}a}{{{a}^{2}}}+\underset{x\to a}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{2\text{cos}\left( \frac{x+a}{2} \right)}{2a}\left[ \frac{\text{sin}\frac{\left( x-a \right)}{2}}{\frac{\left( x-a \right)}{2}} \right]=-\frac{\text{sin}a}{{{a}^{2}}}+\frac{\text{cos}a}{a}$

Illustration

(a) If $f\left( x \right)=\frac{tan2x-x}{3x-sinx}$, find $\underset{x\to 0}{\mathop{Lt}}\,f\left( x \right)$ 
(b) $L{{t}_{x\to 0}}\frac{sin\left( \alpha +\beta \right)x+sin\left( \alpha -\beta \right)x+sin2\alpha x}{cos2\beta x-cos2\alpha x}$. x 
(c) \(L{{t}_{h\to 0}}\frac{{{(a+h)}^{2}}sin\left( a+h \right)-{{a}^{2}}sina}{h}\)
(d) $L{{t}_{x\to 0}}\frac{1-cosxcos2xcos3x}{si{{n}^{2}}x}$ 

Solution: 

(a) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}f\left( x \right)=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\text{tan}2x-x}{3x-\text{sin}x}$ [IIT-71]

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\left( \frac{\text{tan}2x}{2x} \right)2x-x}{3x-\left( \frac{\text{sin}x}{x} \right)x}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\left( \frac{\text{tan}2x}{2x} \right)2-1}{3-\frac{\text{sin}x}{x}}=\frac{2-1}{3-1}=\frac{1}{2}$

(b) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\text{sin}\left( \alpha +\beta \right)x+\text{sin}\left( \alpha -\beta \right)x+\text{sin}2\alpha x}{\text{cos}2\beta x-\text{cos}2\alpha x}\cdot x$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\text{sin}\left( \alpha +\beta \right)x+\text{sin}\left( \alpha -\beta \right)x+\text{sin}2\alpha x}{2\text{sin}\left( \alpha +\beta \right)x\cdot \text{sin}\left( \alpha -\beta \right)x}\cdot x$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\left[ \frac{\frac{\text{sin}\left( \alpha +\beta \right)x}{\left( \alpha +\beta \right)x}\cdot \left( \alpha +\beta \right)x+\frac{\text{sin}\left( \alpha +\beta \right)x}{\left( \alpha -\beta \right)x}\cdot \left( \alpha -\beta \right)x+\frac{\text{sin}2\alpha x}{2\alpha x}\cdot 2\alpha x}{2\frac{\text{sin}\left( \alpha +\beta \right)x}{\left( \alpha +\beta \right)x}\cdot \left( \alpha +\beta \right)x\cdot \frac{\text{sin}\left( \alpha -\beta \right)x}{\left( \alpha -\beta \right)x}\cdot \left( \alpha -\beta \right)x} \right]x$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\frac{\text{sin}\left( \alpha +\beta \right)x}{\left( \alpha +\beta \right)x}\cdot \left( \alpha +\beta \right)+\frac{\text{sin}\left( \alpha -\beta \right)x}{\left( \alpha -\beta \right)x}\cdot \left( \alpha -\beta \right)+\frac{\text{sin}2\alpha x}{2\alpha x}\cdot 2\alpha }{2\frac{\text{sin}\left( \alpha +\beta \right)x}{\left( \alpha +\beta \right)x}\cdot \left( \alpha +\beta \right)\cdot \frac{\text{sin}\left( \alpha -\beta \right)x}{\left( \alpha -\beta \right)x}\cdot \left( \alpha -\beta \right)}$

$=\frac{1\cdot \left( \alpha +\beta \right)+1\cdot \left( \alpha -\beta \right)+1\cdot 2\alpha }{2\cdot 1\left( \alpha +\beta \right)1\cdot \left( \alpha -\beta \right)}=\frac{4\alpha }{2\left( {{\alpha }^{2}}-{{\beta }^{2}} \right)}=\frac{2\alpha }{{{\alpha }^{2}}-{{\beta }^{2}}}$

$=\alpha \text{cos}\alpha +\text{sin}\alpha =\text{sin}\alpha -\alpha \text{cos}\alpha $

(c) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{h}\to 0}}\frac{{{(\text{a}+\text{h})}^{2}}\text{sin}\left( \text{a}+\text{h} \right)-{{\text{a}}^{2}}\text{sina}}{\text{h}}$ [IIT-79]

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{h\to 0}}\frac{\left( {{a}^{2}}+2ah+{{h}^{2}} \right)\text{sin}\left( a+h \right)-{{a}^{2}}\text{sin}a}{h}$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{h\to 0}}\left[ \frac{{{a}^{2}}(\text{sin}\left( a+h \right)-\text{sin}a}{h} \right]+\frac{\left( 2ah+{{h}^{2}} \right)\text{sin}\left( a+h \right)}{h}$

\(\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{h\to 0}}\left[ \frac{{{\text{a}}^{2}}\cdot 2\text{cos}\frac{2\text{a}+\text{h}}{2}\text{sin}\frac{\text{h}}{2}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}}+\left( 2\text{a}+\text{h} \right)\text{sin}\left( \text{a}+\text{h} \right) \right] \\ \end{array}\)

\(~=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{h\to 0}}\left[ \frac{2{{a}^{2}}\text{cos}\frac{2\text{a}+\text{h}}{2}\frac{\text{sin}\frac{\text{h}}{2}}{\frac{h}{2}}\frac{h}{2}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}}+\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{h\to 0}}\left( 2\text{a}+\text{h} \right)\text{sin}\left( \text{a}+\text{h} \right) \right]={{a}^{2}}\cos a+2a\sin a\)

(d) $\text{cos }x\cos 2x\cos 3x$

$=\frac{1}{2}\left( \text{cos}2x\text{cos}3x\text{cos}2x \right)$

$=\frac{1}{2}\left[ \left( \text{cos}2x+\text{cos}4x \right)\text{cos}2x \right]$

$=\frac{1}{4}\left[ 2\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}2x+2\text{cos}4x\text{cos}2x \right]$

$=\frac{1}{4}\left[ 1+\text{cos}4x+\text{cos}2x+\text{cos}6x \right]$

Now $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{1-\text{cos}x\text{cos}2x\text{cos}3x}{\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}2x}$

$~=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{1-\text{cos}2x+1-\text{cos}4x+1-\text{cos}6x}{4\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}2x}$

$~=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{2\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}x+2\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}+2\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}3x}{4\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}2x}$

$~=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{2{{\left( \frac{\text{sin}x}{x} \right)}^{2}}\cdot {{x}^{2}}+2{{\left( \frac{\text{sin}2x}{2x} \right)}^{2}}\cdot 4{{x}^{2}}+2{{\left( \frac{\text{sin}3x}{3x} \right)}^{2}}\cdot 9{{x}^{2}}}{4{{\left( \frac{\text{sin}2x}{2x} \right)}^{2}}\cdot 4{{x}^{2}}}=\frac{28}{16}=\frac{7}{4}$

concepts.title: Solved problems on Trigonometric Limits in which the independent variable tends to ∞ or -∞
concepts.conceptBody:
Trigonometric Problems in which variable tends to infinity

There are no formulas as such for this type
(a) $\underset{x\to \infty }{\mathop{Lt}}\,\left( \frac{sinx}{x} \right)$ 
(b) $\underset{x\to \infty }{\mathop{Lt}}\,2xtan\left( \frac{1}{x} \right)$ 

Solution: 

(a) We have $\underset{x\to \infty }{\mathop{\text{Lt}}}\,\frac{\text{sin}x}{x}$

we know $-1\le \text{sin}x\le 1$, for all values of $x\in R\And $ and as $x\to \infty ,\frac{1}{x}\to 0$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Limit be comes

$\text{Lt}\left( \to 0 \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (a }\!\!~\!\!\text{ number }\!\!~\!\!\text{ between }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( -1,1 \right)$

= 0

Note: You can though remember this limit. 

(b) $\underset{\text{x}\to \infty }{\mathop{\text{Lt}}}\,2\text{xtan}\left( \frac{1}{\text{x}} \right)$

put $\text{x}=\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ so as $\text{x}\to \infty \text{h}\to 0$

$\underset{\text{h}\to 0}{\mathop{\text{Lt}}}\,\frac{2}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}}\text{tan}\left( \text{h} \right)$

= 2 $\left. \left\{ as\underset{h\to 0}{\mathop{\text{Lt}}}\,\frac{\text{tan}h}{h}=1 \right. \right\}$

 

Q) Find the Limit, \[\underset{x\to \infty }{\mathop{Lt}}\,\left( \frac{x+cosx}{x+sinx} \right)\] 

Solution: 

Given Limit is $\underset{x\to \infty }{\mathop{\text{Lt}}}\,\frac{x+\text{cos}x}{x+\text{sin}x}$

$~=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to \infty }}\frac{1+\frac{\text{cos}x}{x}}{1+\frac{\text{sin}x}{x}}$

$=\operatorname{Lt}_{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1+0}{1+0} \quad\left\{\operatorname{Lt}_{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\cos x}{x}=0 \text { using the same concept as in } \frac{\sin x}{x}\right\}$

$~=1.$

concepts.title: Solution by Substituting the variable in the Limit
concepts.conceptBody:
Solved examples

Evaluate the following limits : 
(a) $\underset{x\to 1}{\mathop{Lt}}\,\left( 1-x \right)tan\frac{\pi x}{2}$ 
(b) $L{{t}_{x\to \pi /2}}\frac{1-sinx}{\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-x \right)cotx}$ 

Solution: 

(a) These type of limits are solved by substituting the limit.

See now in the limit,

$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 1}}\left( 1-x \right)\text{tan}\frac{\pi x}{2},\text{tan}\frac{\pi x}{2}$ approaches $\infty $ so somehow we need to remove this. If it can be converted to $\text{cot}\frac{\pi \text{x}}{2}$, then the limit will be solved as $\text{cot}\frac{\pi \text{x}}{2}$ will approach 0 .

And we know $\text{cot}\frac{\pi \text{x}}{2}=\text{tan}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-\frac{\pi }{2}\text{x} \right)$ or $\text{tan}\frac{\pi }{2}\left( 1-\text{x} \right)$

Now, do you see something

putting $\text{x}$ as $\left( 1-\text{x} \right)$ solves the question

$\left( 1-\text{x} \right)$ becomes $\text{x}\And \text{lim}$ changes to

$\underset{x\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,x\text{cot}\frac{\pi x}{2}=\underset{x\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{x}{\text{sin}\frac{\pi x}{2}}=\frac{2}{\pi }\left[ \text{ as }\text{ cos}\theta =1 \right]$

(b) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{x}\to \pi /2}}\frac{1-\text{sinx}}{\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-\text{x} \right)\text{cotx}}$

Note: In these type of questions you will get the clue of what to substitute from the question itself.

Like in this question we will substitute $\frac{\pi }{2}-\text{x}$ for $\text{x}$, hence limit becomes

$\therefore \underset{x\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{1-\text{sin}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-x \right)}{x\text{cot}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-x \right)}=\underset{x\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{1-\text{cos}x}{x\text{tan}x}$

$=\underset{x\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\left( \frac{1-\text{cos}x}{{{x}^{2}}} \right)\left( \frac{x}{\text{tan}x} \right)=\frac{1}{2}\times 1\left\{ \text{as }\left. \underset{x\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{1-\text{cos}x}{{{x}^{2}}}=\frac{1}{2} \right\} \right.$

you can learn this

examples.title: If $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} k x \operatorname{cosec} x=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} x \operatorname{coseckx}$, then $k=$
